questions.question: How do you create a brand new workspace in Notion?

options.text: Click 'Add another account' in the workspace switcher.
options.explanation: The 'Add another account' button is used to log in with a different email address, not to create a workspace.

options.text: Go to Settings and select 'Create Workspace' from the General tab.
options.explanation: Workspaces are created directly from the workspace switcher menu, not from the general settings page.

options.text: Click the 'Join or create workspace' button and scroll past the list of existing workspaces.
options.explanation: This is the correct procedure: open the switcher, click the menu next to your email, and select the option to scroll past existing lists to create a new one.

options.text: Ask an admin from an existing workspace to add you to a new one.
options.explanation: Asking an admin is how you join an *existing* workspace, but it does not create a brand new one.

questions.question: Who is eligible to join a workspace automatically using the 'Join or create workspace' button?

options.text: Anyone with a personal email address like Gmail or Outlook.
options.explanation: Personal emails like Gmail or Outlook do not support the auto-join feature for other workspaces.

options.text: Anyone with a school email address like nyu.edu.
options.explanation: School emails (like nyu.edu) do not support the auto-join feature for other workspaces.

options.text: Users whose email domain is allowed by the workspace's admin settings.
options.explanation: Auto-join is enabled for specific email domains. If your domain is in the 'Allowed Email Domains' list, you can join automatically.

options.text: Users who have previously been invited by an admin.
options.explanation: Users invited by an admin receive a direct link to join; auto-join is a separate feature based on domain settings.

questions.question: What is the keyboard shortcut to switch between workspaces on a desktop?

options.text: Command + Shift + #
options.explanation: The Mac equivalent is Command, but the article specifies the Windows shortcut.

options.text: Ctrl + Shift + #
options.explanation: On desktop, the shortcut is Ctrl + Shift + #, where # corresponds to the position of the workspace in the dropdown menu.

options.text: Alt + Tab
options.explanation: Alt + Tab is a system-wide application switcher, not a Notion workspace switcher shortcut.

options.text: Space + #
options.explanation: There is no specific keyboard shortcut involving Space to switch workspaces.

questions.question: What happens to your data if you leave a workspace?

options.text: The data is moved to a 'Private' section in your main account.
options.explanation: Leaving a workspace removes you from it entirely. Your data is not moved to a Private section; you simply lose access.

options.text: You lose access to all content inside the workspace immediately.
options.explanation: Once you leave, you lose access to all content. You should export data beforehand if you want to keep it.

options.text: The workspace is deleted from the server and cannot be recovered.
options.explanation: The workspace remains on the server, but you are removed from it. You can ask an admin to re-add you if you regain access.

options.text: The admin is automatically notified to delete the workspace for you.
options.explanation: Leaving is a personal action. The admin is not automatically notified to delete the workspace.

questions.question: How can you log out of a single account while staying logged into other accounts simultaneously?

options.text: Use the 'Log out all' button at the bottom of the workspace switcher.
options.explanation: The 'Log out all' button logs out of every account you are currently signed into.

options.text: Go to Settings and select 'Log out' next to that specific account.
options.explanation: The text specifies using the '•••' button (the three dots) next to the account name in the switcher, not the Settings menu.

options.text: Click the '•••' button to the right of the account you want to log out of.
options.explanation: This is the correct method: use the three-dot menu next to the specific account to log out just that one.

options.text: You cannot log out of individual accounts; you must log out of all at once.
options.explanation: Notion explicitly supports logging out of individual accounts using the '•••' button.

questions.question: Why might you want to add another email account to your workspace switcher?

options.text: To ensure all your workspaces are associated with the same email.
options.explanation: Adding another account allows you to use *different* emails for different workspaces (e.g., personal vs. work).

options.text: To increase the speed of Notion loading.
options.explanation: Adding too many accounts can actually slow down performance.

options.text: To keep work and life separate without constantly logging out and back in.
options.explanation: This is the primary benefit: you can access content from multiple accounts seamlessly without logging out.

options.text: To automatically merge content from one workspace into another.
options.explanation: Merging content is done via the 'Move to' function, not by adding an account.

questions.question: How do you move top-level pages to a different workspace?

options.text: Click 'Move to' in the top-level pages' sidebar context menu.
options.explanation: You click the '•••' menu next to a top-level page and select 'Move to', then choose the destination workspace.

options.text: Drag and drop the page into the sidebar of another workspace.
options.explanation: Drag and drop is not mentioned as a method; you must use the context menu.

options.text: Copy the page and paste it into the new workspace.
options.explanation: While you can copy, the 'Move to' function specifically handles the transfer of sub-pages as well.

options.text: Ask an admin to move the page for you.
options.explanation: Users can move their own top-level pages without admin intervention.

questions.question: What is the validity period of login tokens in Notion?

options.text: 30 days
options.explanation: Tokens expire after 90 days, not 30.

options.text: 60 days
options.explanation: Tokens expire after 90 days, not 60.

options.text: 90 days
options.explanation: Login tokens expire after 90 days. If this happens, you must use 'Add another account' to re-authenticate.

options.text: Indefinitely, until you manually log out.
options.explanation: Tokens have a finite lifespan of 90 days.

questions.question: Which of the following is a restriction on Paid Plans?

options.text: You can link content between different workspaces.
options.explanation: Workspaces are completely separate silos; you cannot link content between them.

options.text: A single paid plan covers all workspaces in your account.
options.explanation: Paid plans only apply to one specific workspace, not the whole account.

options.text: You can migrate content between workspaces without issues.
options.explanation: Migrated content is duplicated and sometimes has issues, regardless of the plan.

options.text: You cannot have more than one workspace on a paid plan.
options.explanation: You can have multiple workspaces, but the paid plan only covers one of them.

questions.question: When moving top-level pages to another workspace, how are the sub-pages handled?

options.text: They are deleted from the original workspace.
options.explanation: Moving a page does not delete it; it remains in the original workspace.

options.text: They are not moved and stay in the original workspace.
options.explanation: The text states that 'all their sub-pages go with them' when moving top-level pages.

options.text: They appear in the 'Private' section of the sidebar after moving.
options.explanation: After moving, they appear in the new workspace. The 'Private' section refers to where moved pages initially land within the new workspace.

options.text: They are duplicated in the new workspace but remain in the original.
options.explanation: Moving a page creates a copy in the new workspace (duplicates content), while the original remains intact.

questions.question: If you are logged into 10 or more accounts simultaneously, what might happen?

options.text: Your accounts will be automatically deleted.
options.explanation: There is no automatic deletion of accounts for having too many.

options.text: You will experience slower performance.
options.explanation: While there is currently no hard limit on the number of accounts, performance can become sluggish when logged into 10+ accounts.

options.text: You will be forced to log out of all accounts.
options.explanation: You are not forced to log out; however, performance may degrade.

options.text: The workspace switcher will stop working.
options.explanation: The switcher should still function, though it may be slower.

questions.question: How can you log out of a device remotely if you no longer have access to your account?

options.text: Send a message to Notion Support via email.
options.explanation: If you lose access to your device, you can contact support via email or use the Notion AI face to log out remotely.

options.text: Use the 'Log out all' button on the public computer you used.
options.explanation: You no longer have access to that computer, so you cannot use the button there.

options.text: Wait for the 90-day token expiration.
options.explanation: Token expiration can take up to 90 days. You should log out immediately for security.

options.text: Change your password in the settings of your current device.
options.explanation: You do not have access to your current device to change settings.
